  • In 2022, I had a positive stance and made a 0.2% investment in Pentair, which since that time has returned over 100,% inclusive of dividends. That makes this a superb investment. However, the time has come to review the fundamentals and potential of the investment.
  • As of Q1'25, I view the company's valuation as too high for a continued investment, or to continue to hold on to the profits in my investment. I am therefore downgrading my rating to "HOLD", and I am taking home profits from the investment. Due to only 6-7% annualized RoR potential conservatively (as I see it), I view the company as not attractive enough here. My entry price target is increased to a PT of $85/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $85/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of June 2025.

data.title: An All AI Portfolio: AI Already Has ROI
data.description:
Learn To Code Will Become "Learn To Prompt"

For all those that have been pondering when AI will produce a positive "ROI" for its CAPEX investments, it's already here, folks.

Here are some headlines:

"AI is Literally Eating These Jobs: Data Shows 76,440 Roles Eliminated by AI in 2025"

"Klarna, UPS, Duolingo Lead the Charge: Major Companies Swiftly Replacing Human Workers with AI"

"Entry-Level Jobs Most at Risk: AI Reshapes Career Ladder, Threatening New Grads"

"From Customer Service to Copywriting: The Jobs AI is Poised to Eliminate in the Near Future"

While firings are not exactly new revenue drivers, they do expand margins and reduce costs. The rise of "agentic AI" is here, and HR departments are quickly learning that many entry-level jobs can be replaced by AI agents. These are the programmable AI avatars that can be prompted to do tasks for you. Be it data entry, legal document production, cold calling sales leads or email blasting potential clients, these things can run for an enterprise 24/7 for the cost of a subscription. No benefits, 401K, sick days or weekends.

A new term has also been coined, "prompt". While in the past, a popular phrase when green energy jobs were being promoted in the US and many blue collar "dirty" energy jobs were about to be axed, politicians said these blue-collar workers should just "learn to code" and get on with their lives. Surely, it's not that easy, but now AI has made it so it can do for you what you will it to. The most important skill in the future will be having the ability to be as exact and descriptive as possible in a paragraph or two when directing an AI assistant.

Having played extensively with most of the popular AI LLMs out there and listened to hours upon hours of interviews with Silicon Valley tech leaders about how these things work, prompting is a whole new skill. An AI system if given an exact enough command can work wonders and accomplish amazing things. The new Google VEO3 (GOOGL)(GOOG) video generator, for instance, can create an ok video when you give it a prompt yourself with a paragraph or so, but much better when first prompting an LLM to make a better prompt.

If you first give that paragraph to an LLM like ChatGPT for instance and say "make me this sort of video and make it scripted for Google's VEO 3 platform at 8 seconds in length", then it spits out a long list of descriptions with sound commands and second by second interjections for sounds and movements. This is the sort of skill set your kids will need in the future, and you may as well.

How I'm playing AI

Experiencing and seeing advanced examples of AI agents makes me believe with even further conviction that the AI data center CAPEX build out will not stop. It will be relentless, and it is now an arms race to see who can first get to artificial general intelligence [AGI] and super-intelligent AI. The kind where AI programs and improves itself without human intervention. This can be thought of along the same lines as compound interest. Let's just hope the end outcome is as rosy rather than dystopian.

The "intangible" asset MAGs

I call this set of companies the "intangible Mags" as they deal with more income producing intangible assets versus tangible. This list includes:

  • Amazon (AMZN)
  • Google
  • Meta (META)
  • Microsoft (MSFT)

The margins are wider when dealing with intellectual property assets and these companies are all producing their own AI integrated systems and incubate or own LLM platforms for which other agents can build their software upon. These are mega cap hyperscalers and the ones driving the actual growth in AI. Without them, there would be nothing as they are footing the primary chunk of CAPEX investment in this movement.

Meta will personalize and automate ad creation, with AI-generated ads targeted at specific demographics and possibly changing depending on the target.

Google One, with its Ultra Plan, now offers eerily realistic sound and video in one prompt on the company's VEO 3 video platform.

Amazon is helping to incubate and move forward Anthropic which has one of the most powerful agentic AI systems in Claude which may be already sentient enough to have self-survival instincts.

Microsoft has the much touted partnership with Open AI and ChatGPT, with a possible 49% stake in any profits that eventually get pumped out of the LLM system when or if it turns a profit.

All of these companies are moving the ball on AI and competing with one another. This is the hegemonic tier of AI development. All are priced fairly, with Google being particularly cheap.

"Agentic" AI exposure

Then we have the next tier down of agentic AI. These companies are either building their own models or utilizing available ones that are working with the hyperscalers. They are not in that Trillion dollar market cap range, but are formidable none-the-less. That list includes:

  • ServiceNow (NOW)
  • Salesforce (CRM)
  • HubSpot (HUBS)
  • UiPath (PATH)
  • IBM (IBM)
  • Panasonic (OTCPK:PCRHY)(OTCPK:PCRFF)

This group typically trades at higher multiples with their smaller market caps and possibly higher upside potential.

ServiceNow is developing their AI Agent Orchestrator to assist with IT and HR aspects of enterprises.

Salesforce is implementing their "Agentforce 2.0" platform to help with all levels of sales, customer service and marketing. I personally believe Salesforce already has the client base demand as a CRM to sell AI agent platforms at a faster scale than others listed here. Helping to replace armies of cold callers, which is what Salesforce caters to in the first place.

HubSpot covers many bases with their agentic AI, from content creation and audience scaling with Breeze AI.

IBM continues to develop their Watson platform for enterprises with WatsonX and Orchestrate. This includes workflow agents, coding agents and CRM platforms.

Panasonic is by far the cheapest of the bunch and a dark horse with their purchase of Blue Yonder. The $7.1 Billion acquisition by Panasonic in 2021 may finally bear fruit, with the company now having a supply chain management AI agent for factories coming online using Azure.

Gas MLPs

Next, these energy hungry data centers need tons of electricity and fast. Larger data centers may need up to 1 gigawatt of electricity, enough to power a small town. Here are some headlines:

AI Boom Fuels "Natural Gas Gold Rush" as Data Centers Seek Reliable Power

Critics Warn: New Gas Plants for Data Centers Risk Locking in Decades of Pollution and Higher Costs

U.S. Data Centers Increasingly Turn to "Behind-the-Meter" Natural Gas Solutions for Energy Independence

While nuclear and small modular reactors have been getting all the attention, I still think it's a pipe dream in the land of "nimbyism". Having grown up in the 80s and 90s, television and movies constantly painted a picture of nuclear dystopia. From the Simpsons to the Toxic Avengers, many of us have grown to fear anything nuclear related. The Chernobyl disaster in 1986 didn't help either.

Natural gas, on the other hand, is piped around the US via natural gas master limited partnerships to [MLPs]. Many are being built right up to data center sites like Elon Musk did for his xAI Colossus project in Memphis, Tennessee. These gas MLPs own the pipes rather than the commodity in general and collect a toll road fee on the volume of gas pumping through. More volume, more distributable cash flows.

Since nuclear takes so long to get up and running, there is little debate that the majority of AI and data center growth will be powered by natural gas. This energy is greener than coal and oil and is being deemed a "cleaner" transition fuel until the world has infrastructure for all renewable energy. I'm betting that natural gas with its reliability will always be a primary fuel for electricity.

Personally, if I want exposure here, give me the WalMarts, Amazons and Costcos of natural gas pipelines, or in short, those with the most revenue:

Energy Transfer (ET) and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) are the giants in this space.

Both sell at extremely reasonable EV/EBITDA ratios [proxy for cash flow] which is the source of their large dividends.

With Energy Transfer having a 7.49% yield and Enterprise Products Partners a 6.94% yield, the MLP space is still one of the better high-yield sections of the market. The other advantage in taxable accounts is the majority of the dividend is return of capital since the pipelines have so much depreciation expense. This lowers your basis and is reported on a separate K1 form for US investors. Return of capital is not immediately taxable but taxed upon the sale of units via a recapturing of your depreciated cost basis.

What I personally plan to do with these is buy while they're cheap and reinvest while they're cheap [DRIP], and turn off the drip if they get too hot and caught up in the AI story as more and more data centers gravitate to their terminals to build. I don't plan to ever sell or own too many due to the extra tax filings. I'm just buying the big boys and kicking back.

Broad chip exposure

Here, I'm just buying either The iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) or VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH). If you don't already have exposure to NVIDIA(NVDA), then SMH might be better for you, as it has an 21.7% weighting in NVIDIA. If you do already own a lot of NVIDIA, I would go with SOXX as that ETF is more broadly diversified with more holdings and only an 8.65% weighting in NVIDIA. Both have been massive total returners:

Can the party keep going? Some say we are still under-hyping AI at the moment.

Risks and summary

These are all the AI plays that I have in my noggin at the moment. No long shots or odd-ball plays. I tend to go with well-known blue chips in my portfolios for the most part. The risk is always that the theme does not play out as anticipated. AI could get regulated as it gets too smart or dangerous, and it could also just run out of power and parts to keep growing at this rate. The hyperscalers could also run out of capital if they're not careful.

For now, we're all going to need to learn how to prompt, promptly.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CrowdStrike Q1 '26: 3 Compelling Reasons To Buy The Dip
data.description:
CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:CRWD) is the world’s second-largest cybersecurity company in terms of market cap - in between Palo Alto Networks (NASDAQ: PANW) and Fortinet (NASDAQ: FTNT).

The company is based in Austin, Texas, and it specializes in protecting clients from cyber threats. CrowdStrike was founded in 2011 by George Kurtz and others and has built a solid reputation by investigating high-profile cyber attacks, like the 2014 Sony Pictures hack and Democratic National Security breaches.

The CrowdStrike Falcon® platform is the company’s core business. The platform has evolved over the years and is today uses artificial intelligence to detect potential security threats and provides clients with real-time notifications. In plain English, the security solution works by installing a small piece of software on devices that continuously scans for suspicious activities. If such activity is found, the client is notified, and attempts are made to block these incoming attacks.

It’s no surprise that CrowdStrike has been growing quite rapidly since its launch - thanks to the increasing number of cybersecurity threats. Now, CrowdStrike employs over 10,000 professionals, serving organizations of all sizes.

Review: CrowdStrike’s Q1’ 26 Financials

On June 3, 2025, CrowdStrike released its Q1 '26 financials.

On a GAAP basis, revenue for the quarter increased almost 20% year-over-year to $1.1 billion, hitting its target guidance. The primary driver of this growth comes from the company’s subscription revenue, reaching $1.05 billion for the quarter, which is up 20% from the same year-ago quarter.

Subscription revenue is one of the most critical metrics for any SaaS company, such as CrowdStrike - and investors should pay attention to it. It's the lifeblood of the business model.

Going back, CrowdStrike’s gross margin for the quarter is 77%, which I consider "world-class" in the cybersecurity space. Although it’s 1% lower than the previous year, the compression is insignificant considering the company’s heavy investments in AI and overall platform optimization. For context, Nvidia’s (NASDAQ: NVDA) GAAP gross margin for its Q1 ’26 was 60.5%, yet the company is still considered highly profitable.

CrowdStrike reported a loss from operations of $124.7 million, which is quite significant compared to the previous year’s operating income of $6.9 million. The loss was attributed to the overall increase in operating expenses and $39.7 million in one-time expenses related to the global IT outage incident back in July 2024 and other related matters.

On the bottom line, CrowdStrike reported a net loss of $110.2 million, or $0.44 per share (diluted), which is also concerning when compared to last year’s net income of $42.8 million. That said, I don't think long-term investors should be worried. The losses appear to be one-time and short-term hiccups, largely caused by the July 19 incident and massive investments in R&D, which increased by 42% year-over-year.

I believe that these investments are positioning CrowdStrike to capitalize on AI-powered cybersecurity, which will translate to improved numbers in future quarters.

CrowdStrike’s 5YR Revenue

As I’ve mentioned before, a picture is worth a thousand words. For CrowdStrike, it’s hard not to believe the hype once we look at their revenue growth from its fiscal year 2018 until today. Spoiler: it’s an impressive growth story in the enterprise software sector: in a span of 7 years, the company grew its revenue a staggering 3,223%.

To be sure, this growth pattern reflects the overall need for cybersecurity. In the years prior to 2021, CrowdStrike was only proving its cloud-native approach against the pioneers in space. However, the real inflection point came post-pandemic when big corporations began adapting to cloud infrastructure and remote work became the "new normal."

Since then, the demand for traditional perimeter-based security declined, while cybersecurity skyrocketed. CrowdStrike customers also started replacing their entire security stack with the Falcon® platform - deepening relationships with the existing clientele and attracting new customers adapting to the new normal.

Given how CrowdStrike managed to capitalize on various industry trends without losing momentum, I expect the company revenue’s trajectory to continue - perhaps even double in the next 5 years - as AI-powered cybersecurity adaption unfolds.

CRWD Stock Price Action

Is the stock price moving in the same pattern as the company’s revenue? On a year-to-date basis, the CRWD stock is up over 40%. Over the past 52 weeks, CRWD stock has traded between $200.81 to $491.20. At the time of writing, the stock last traded at $488.76 - fairly close to its 52-week high. However, before the bell, the stock is trading about 7% lower.

Personally, I don't think there will be significant price appreciation in the following weeks. Rather, I expect that CrowdStrike’s Q1 ‘26 financials will weed out more short-term investors, allowing a growth runway to present itself.

I mean, increased volatility around following earnings is the norm CrowdStrike, so long-term investors shouldn’t panic. Just look at CRWD’s 1-YR price chart:

As you can see, there’s been a handful of dips for CRWD stock over the past 1 year - especially after the July 19 incident and CrowdStrike’s FY ‘25 financials - but even so, the stock is up by 59% on a 1-YR basis, which tells me that investors (institutional and the wise ones) see the real strength and potential of the business despite momentary hiccups.

The most dramatic selloff followed the global IT outage, sending shares plummeting from around $400 to below $220 in just a matter of days, but it was just a classic reaction when fear completely overwhelmed the fundamentals. That said, the stock quickly recovered as sophisticated investors took the opportunity to buy the dip.

The point is, I’m expecting CRWD stock to decline in the short term, particularly due to its significantly negative earnings, and will translate to the next perfect opportunity to buy a position at a discount and hold it long-term.

Don’t believe it? CRWD stock’s 5-YR price action shows how generous it’s been to long-term investors:

Although there have been notable dips and selloffs over the past five years, the CRWD stock has always bounced back stronger.

In fact, the stock soared over 400% during the period despite high volatility - this is what you can expect from a high-growth tech company with realistic long-term prospects and solid business fundamentals.

5-YR Price Return Comparison

Speaking of price appreciation, I wanted to compare CRWD’s 5-year price return with its closest industry peers:

CRWD is the orange line, and looking at the chart, you’d get how volatile the market is and how fierce the competition is.

CrowdStrike’s nearly 400% return over the period is surely impressive, but how it got there is what interests me more. The stock weathered the 2022-2023 downturn with less volatility than Palo Alto and Fortinet. The market was dragging down high-growth tech stocks, but CrowdStrike business fundamentals kept it from experiencing those violent swings that hammered most of its peers.

Another interesting aspect is that CrowdStrike isn’t just riding a single momentum: it’s been capitalizing on various trends. In the chart above, you can see multiple notable growth phases for the stock - the initial climb during 2021, the consolidation during broader tech correction, and its strong bounce back starting late 2023.

CrowdStrike's most notable peers are Palo Alto Networks and Fortinet. They have the highest price return rates, and they’ve also gained momentum through multiple corrections, but CrowdStrike is certainly not falling behind. In fact, the gap between Palo Alto and CrowdStrike is quite narrow.

As a long-term investor, what gives me confidence with CrowdStrike is that its outperformance coincides with its actually-improving business fundamentals. It’s got higher margins, better cash generation, and is pouring hundreds of millions into AI and research and development. The stock performance and operational metrics are moving in the same direction within the same timeframe, and that says a lot about the company’s long-term potential.

3 Reasons To Be Bullish On CrowdStrike

While doing a thorough analysis of CrowdStrike’s Q1 ’26 financials, I spotted some notable catalysts that could significantly affect the company’s trajectory and investor returns - and these are crucial since we’re investing in the company for its growth potential.

Falcon Flex's Growth Engine

Let me start with what I consider the most undervalued catalyst in CrowdStrike’s arsenal right now, which is the Falcon Flex. The licensing agreement reached $3.2 billion, up 600% year-over-year. That said, the explosive growth of this subscription-based model isn’t just impressive from a numbers standpoint. This is actually single-handedly changing how enterprises think about investing in cybersecurity solutions.

I think many of us don’t understand why Falcon Flex is so important right now. You see, for decades, procuring cybersecurity solutions has been a nightmare for vendors, buyers, and customers alike.

Sometimes, you’d get these rigid, multi-year contracts that took months to negotiate. If you run out of luck, legal teams would be fighting over every clause, and enterprises would be forced to stick to security solutions that might not even address their current needs. It was broken, and frankly, it was far too slow compared to the pace at which cyberattacks move.

That said, Falcon Flex can eliminate those dilemmas. Customers can maximize their security solutions with a licensing agreement that’s fully customizable to their needs. In practical terms, it means enterprises can essentially pre-purchase a bucket of CrowdStrike credits and deploy various security modules as the “needs” change - without going through those lengthy procurement processes.

Here’s something for investors. CrowdStrike announced a 97% gross retention rate and reports Falcon Flex adding over $1 billion of in-quarter deal value, which means customers are increasingly establishing Falcon Platform as their AI-native SOC. This 97% retention rate can be attributed to customer satisfaction, but that’s not all about it - this is a sign of widespread adoption.

Once clients experience the effectiveness of managing their security through a single platform with a flexible deployment, no one’s stopping them from consolidating other security tools into CrowdStrike - it creates a compounding effect over time. To be clear, that’s not a claim or a theory. It’s already happening. Rate Companies chose to adopt the CrowdStrike solutions and scale it to user licenses without those pesky negotiation contracts.

Charlotte AI and Agentic Workflows

The next potential catalyst for CrowdStrike is their deployment of Charlotte AI, but not for the reason that most people consider. I mean, sure, everyone’s talking about widespread AI adaptation across industries, including cybersecurity, but CrowdStrike isn't another chatbot that’s added onto their platform. It’s something far more sophisticated - and highly valuable.

This is going to be a bit of a "tech-talk" but follow me for a moment. Charlotte AI is fundamentally different because its underlying AI models are tuned on Falcon platform documentation and CrowdStrike’s very own high-fidelity security telemetry. This is critical because it means Charlotte isn’t running on generic data from unknown data centers - Charlotte AI is trained on specific security datasets that CrowdStrike accumulated from protecting thousands of organizations all over the world. That alone makes Charlotte AI one of a kind.

More importantly, Charlotte AI is being continuously trained on the latest investigation guidance from CrowdStrike Falcon Complete MDR's security experts and detection triage data. This is what I call the "human-to-AI feedback loop," making the trained model smarter and stronger over time. Each time CrowdStrike’s threat hunters investigate an incident, or whenever they discover new cyber attack techniques, all of those will be fed to Charlotte AI.

Honestly, this is quite impressive and presents huge growth potential for CrowdStrike, but at this point - as someone who understands how agentic AI works - I somehow find this terrifying. But maybe that’s just me.

Capital Allocation Strategy

This next catalyst might seem "stating the obvious," but hear me out as it actually demonstrates the company’s market position and the executive’s confidence in their overall trajectory.

Earlier this month, CrowdStrike announced a $1 billion allocation to repurchase shares. It made me think, is that some sort of financial engineering exercise? Not exactly. It appears to be almost an ‘out-of-confidence’ play. Let me put this buyback into context so we can better understand.

CrowdStrike’s Cash and Cash Equivalents in its Q1 ‘26 report increased to a record $4.61 billion, which gives the company the financial muscle. But what’s more impressive is the company’s cash generation rates that allowed it to be in the position - the net cash generated from operations was a record $384 million for the quarter alone. If you annualize that figure, you’d be looking at $1.5 billion in operating cash flow, and this is what made the $1 billion buyback program possible and conservative.

Despite dealing with the July 19 incident - which cost them nearly $40 million - the management is still fairly confident in returning significant capital to shareholders. It makes sense, given that the incident was a one-off operational challenge, not something that hit the business beyond repair.

To sum it up, investors know how CrowdStrike management deals with crises, and for that, I rate them an A+. And it’s crucial because CrowdStrike operates in a market with peers that burn cash without a clear path to profitability. However, the company is generating substantial cash flow while growing rapidly, which is one of the biggest reasons why I'm a CRWD bull.

My Verdict

Cybersecurity attacks instill fear in enterprises. CrowdStrike found a way to mitigate the threats, using AI to protect enterprises. And I think that's just the beginning. My guess is that we’ll be seeing fully AI-powered security systems in the future, and companies like CrowdStrike will be sitting in the driver’s seat.

For long-term investors like myself, CrowdStrike’s historical performance alone would justify my "Strong Buy" rating on CRWD stock. Not only that, the company’s catalysts are compelling enough for someone who’s been speculating the AI space for some time to double down on CrowdStrike.

Market Overview

The first quarter ushered in a change of US leadership and with it a change in global equity market dynamics.

Though 2025 started well for risk assets broadly, sour sentiment took hold of US markets in mid-February as concerns mounted about the economic impacts of the Trump administration’s policy priorities.

Meanwhile, non-US markets appeared to be reinvigorated by plans for greater fiscal spending in Germany and China. In contrast with recent US dominance, the MSCI EAFE Index outperformed the S&P 500 Index handily during the first quarter, 6.9% to -4.3%, while US exposure drove a 1.8% decline in the MSCI World Index. In another trend reversal, value outperformed growth across markets.1

Of course, circumstances changed dramatically in the early days of the second quarter, as we will discuss later in this report.

Sentiment Journey

Trump’s election victory in November 2024 initially fueled another leg higher in equities, as markets hoped the campaign trail promises of pro-growth deregulation and stimulative tax policy would bolster earnings for a range of US companies amid steady economic growth and cooling inflation. This optimism began to fade well before Trump took office in late January, however, and the unraveling of the so-called “Trump trade” was underway in earnest by mid-February as it became clear that the new administration’s policy timeline prioritized tariffs and program cuts over stimulative measures. It wasn’t long before domestic stock markets had given back all of their post-election day gains and then some.2

Non-US markets were mostly unfazed, however, buoyed by the prospect of increased fiscal spending. Germany’s new governing coalition spearheaded legislation to permanently exempt defense spending above 1% of GDP from its restrictive constitutional debt brake and created a €500 billion infrastructure fund, allowing the European country with the most fiscal space the flexibility to deploy it.3 Earlier, the annual meeting of China’s parliament emphasized the need to boost consumption in the face of external threats like the escalating trade war with the US.4 This comes on top of plans to increase fiscal support by 1 to 2% of GDP this year.

In the US, hard economic data released in the first quarter continued to show persistent economic growth alongside stubbornly above-target inflation in the US. However, soft indicators appeared to capture the early negative impacts of the new administration’s policy erraticism and aggressive cost cutting. Consumers, for one, have grown decidedly more cautious in the face of the prevailing uncertainty. Consumer confidence as measured by The Conference Board has declined for four consecutive months, and its expectations index—which captures consumers’ short-term outlook for income, business and labor-market conditions—fell to a 12-year low in March and sits well below the threshold that usually signals coming recession.5 A survey from the University of Michigan indicated similarly crumbling sentiment,6 while the New York Fed’s latest consumer data revealed notable pessimism across prospects for household finances, employment, loan delinquencies and credit access.7

Business attitudes have also darkened. On Main Street USA, the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index depicted waning confidence in the economy and high and rising uncertainty in what the future holds.8 Globally, business confidence in the year ahead approached post-Covid lows in March, with US manufacturing Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) slipping to below 50 indicating expectations of contracting activity.9 Perhaps most tellingly, tariffs were mentioned more than 800 times in investor events or conference calls during the first quarter, the highest rate in 15 years.10 With this level of concern evident even before the announcement of Trump’s tariff plan, it seems likely the topic will dominate earnings calls in the weeks ahead.

Mind the Gap

While the environment has changed dramatically in the early days of the second quarter—more on that later—it’s worth noting that the US economy entered the year with an ongoing positive output gap, as well as below-average unemployment and above-average wage growth. A positive output gap indicates the economy is operating above potential, and the resulting inflationary impulse typically invites a cooling response from policymakers. For example, the Federal Reserve’s rate hikes in 2022 and 2023 in response to persistently high inflation narrowed but did not close the output gap. With monetary policy settings currently pretty close to neutral, the persistence of the positive output gap likely can be attributed to expansionary fiscal policy and ongoing deficit spending.11

Persistent deficits have also helped support corporate earnings, in our view, and skewed risk perception in US markets. From World War II through the global financial crisis, the country’s primary fiscal position was more or less in balance through decades of offsetting deficits and surpluses. Since 2009, however, economic cycles have been characterized by deeper primary deficits at the bottom and shallower deficits at the top.12 The level of S&P 500 earnings has more or less kept pace with the mounting pile of debt that has resulted from persistent deficit spending for much of the twenty-first century.13

Which is to say that as government debt goes, so too goes the nominal drift in the economy and corporate participation in it. Assuming that current levels of debt accumulation are needed to support expectations of continued earnings growth, a reacceleration in payroll growth, wage growth and inflation are plausible—though underappreciated—byproducts.

Between a Rock and a Hard Place

While it’s painless to acknowledge the structural deterioration of the country’s fiscal profile, fixing it is not. Easy fiscal policy begets difficult choices: Do nothing to contain spending and increase the risk of inflation, or take action to curb it and increase the risk of recession.

Federal outlays as a percentage of GDP have been chronically high since the onset of Covid-19 and amounted to around 23% in fiscal 2024.14 Dividing federal spending into its three major components— discretionary, net interest and mandatory—can help illuminate the challenges facing policymakers seeking to address the issue.

Discretionary spending comprises nondefense and defense spending and is funded annually through the congressional appropriations process. Nondefense discretionary spending supports a wide range of programs, from education and public health to scientific research and tax collections. While the Trump administration has identified nondefense discretionary spending as the lowest-hanging and most politically expedient fruit to pick, at 3.3% of GDP, it’s a small harvest.

So while the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) makes headlines by taking a chainsaw to these outlays, its ability to impact the deficit and debt is minimal. For example, while a 20% cut to the nondefense discretionary budget would be significant to the programs impacted, it would only amount to about 0.6% of GDP.

On the defense side, spending has shifted markedly lower since the end of the Cold War and in 2024 comprised just 2.9% of GDP, slightly above the all-time low of 2.7% in 1999. Given the shifting alliances amid an increasingly shaky geopolitical landscape, it’s hard to imagine this rate going lower. The Trump administration has urged its allies in the North Atlantic Treaty Organization to increase their defense spending in the face of new and emerging threats, and Secretary of State Rubio has signaled that the US would follow suit.15 Trump himself recently announced that he plans to request $1 trillion for defense in the fiscal 2026 budget, up from about $850 billion in 2025.16

Assuming a unilateral restructuring or voluntary default of US debt is off the table, net interest spending is untouchable. The cost of servicing government debt has climbed rapidly in recent years as sub-2% debt issued during the Covid-19 period matured and was replaced by debt at rates in excess of 4%; this trend is forecast to continue.17 Notably, 2024 represented the first year that net interest spending exceeded defense spending. “Ferguson’s Law” posits that any power that spends more on debt servicing than on defense faces a heightened risk of imperial decline. With the US in violation of this law, there is perhaps some inevitability to the country’s pullback from the global stage.18

With both discretionary and net interest spending unlikely sources of relief, meaningful progress on US debt likely will require entitlement reform and/or higher revenue. The bulk of government outlays— representing 14.1% of GDP in 2024—are directed toward programs like Social Security, Medicare and Medicaid, mandatory commitments whose parameters are enshrined into law. Mustering the necessary political will for changes to programs so broadly popular with voters seems like an insurmountable challenge. Generating additional revenue through higher taxes also appears difficult to reach with Republicans controlling the White House and both chambers of Congress.

Enter tariffs. Charged to importers at the port of entry into the US and mostly passed along to end consumers, tariffs act as a sort of backdoor consumption tax on Americans. Yes, they generate revenue for the federal government, but at the likely cost of higher prices and slower economic activity.

Trump championed the benefits of tariffs throughout his presidential campaign, and he quickly—if with shifting degrees of conviction— slapped new levies on specific countries (notably, Canada, Mexico and China) and industries (steel and aluminum) upon taking office.

Designating April 2 as “Liberation Day,” Trump appeared in the White House’s Rose Garden to introduce a wide-ranging package of tariffs, imposing a baseline 10% charge on all imports globally and steeper rates—referred to as, but not actually, “reciprocal”—on countries deemed to be “bad actors.”19

More extreme than markets seemed to anticipate, the tariff announcement unleashed a rout across risk assets worldwide and a significant spike in volatility. Perhaps of greater concern was the selloff in Treasuries and the US dollar, which may suggest wavering conviction in these assets as reliable “safe havens” during periods of unrest. A week later, via social media and with decidedly less fanfare, Trump announced that while the 10% baseline tariff would continue, the “reciprocal” tariff would be paused for all countries except China, which is now subject to a tariff of 145%.20

Seeking Ballast amid the Chaos

We make a point of acknowledging our inability to forecast the future even in the most placid of times, but Trump’s to and fro approach to trade policy—which thus far has included tariffs, counter-tariffs, counter-counter-tariffs, tariff pauses, etc.—has introduced profound instability to the global order.

Given the lack of both historical comparisons and clarity around many important variables, directionality may be the best one can hope for in a forecast. To that end, the global tariff framework as currently designed likely would raise prices and slow economic activity in the US while weighing on both prices and economic growth abroad. Further, tariffs and their indeterminate impact reflect another investment risk in a world rife with them, the aggregate impact of which suggests a greater likelihood of nonlinear market moves such as those we have seen in recent days.

But rather than making concentrated bets on the direction of markets, we continue to focus on investing in a diversified basket of individual assets that we believe have the potential to demonstrate resilience across multiple states of the world. In certain portfolios, this includes gold, whose resilience year-to-date underscores why we advocate for a strategic exposure to the metal as a potential hedge against adverse market outcomes. Gold also serves as a source of long-term deferred purchasing power, easily convertible to cash when market dislocations produce opportunities to invest in high-quality businesses at prices that have suddenly become much more attractive.

Portfolio Review

Overseas Fund A Shares (without sales charge*) posted a return of 10.92% in first quarter 2025. All regions contributed to performance; developed Europe and North America were the leading contributors while developed Asia excluding Japan and Japan lagged. All equity sectors contributed to performance; materials, consumer staples and financials were the largest contributors while information technology, real estate and health care lagged. The Overseas Fund outperformed the MSCI EAFE Index in the period.

Leading contributors in the First Eagle Overseas Fund this quarter included gold bullion, Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. (BABA)(OTCPK:BABAF), Imperial Oil Limited (IMO), BAE Systems plc (OTCPK:BAESF)(OTCPK:BAESY) and Compagnie Financière Richemont SA (OTCPK:CFRHF)(OTCPK:CFRUY).

Gold continued to serve its role as a potential hedge against adverse market conditions during the quarter, setting a series of new nominal highs as the dollar slumped and real interest rates fell. Sustained geopolitical unrest continued to underpin demand from central banks, as did the potential fallout from a shifting and uncertain global trade framework.

Chinese technology giant Alibaba reported strong earnings during the quarter, reflecting robust e-commerce and cloud operations. With the dominant cloud infrastructure business in China, Alibaba should be positioned to benefit from Chinese investment in generative artificial intelligence (AI). Notably, Apple (AAPL) has selected Alibaba to develop AI features for its phones in China. Alibaba continues to invest in growing its businesses and improving operating efficiencies, even as it returns cash to shareholders through dividends and stock repurchases.

Imperial Oil is a Canadian integrated oil company that is 70% owned by Exxon Mobil (XOM). Record production drove strong earnings and cash flows during the quarter, with higher realizations as increased pipeline capacity narrowed the discount for Canadian producers transporting product to the US Gulf. With low cash-production costs and diversification into downstream markets, Imperial should be positioned to withstand prospectively lower commodity prices. Its concentration of assets across just a few key sites moderates Imperial’s need for reinvestment, and management has demonstrated prudent stewardship of capital—with a strong record of returning cash to shareholders through both buybacks and dividends.

BAE Systems is the largest defense contractor in the UK. The company is well positioned to benefit from prospectively easier fiscal policies in Europe as countries seek to increase their defense spending in the face of uncertain military support from the US. BAE’s long-term government contracts generate recurring revenues and provide cyclical resilience.

Swiss luxury goods company Richemont (OTCPK:CFRHF)(OTCPK:CFRUY)—with maisons that include Cartier and Van Cleef & Arpels—rallied during the quarter. The stock was buoyed by reports of a smaller-than-expected decline in Asia–Pacific even as demand in China—which accounts for one-third of the company’s sales—remained challenging. Double-digit sales growth in the Americas, Europe, Middle East/Africa and Japan also helped. Richemont’s diversified portfolio of storied brands, combined with a robust balance sheet, underpin the company’s secular and cyclical resilience.

The leading detractors in the quarter were Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., Ltd. Sponsored ADR (TSM), Diageo plc (DEO)(OTCPK:DGEAF), Sodexo SA (OTCPK:SDXOF)(OTCPK:SDXAY), Henkel AG & Co. KGaA Pref (OTCPK:HENKY)(OTCPK:HENOY)(OTCPK:HELKF) and Bakkafrost P/F (OTCPK:BKFKF)(OTCPK:BKFKY).

Taiwan Semiconductor is the world’s largest semiconductor foundry, and includes Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Intel (INTC), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Apple among its clients. As a primary manufacturer of advanced chips used in generative artificial intelligence, the company has seen continued strong sales in this category. However, its stock traded down due to concerns about the potential impact of tariffs on its business. The company has made significant efforts in recent years to diversify its manufacturing base geographically and recently announced plans to increase its manufacturing capacity in the US.

Domiciled in the UK, Diageo is a multinational alcoholic beverage company with a portfolio of brands, including Guiness, Johnnie Walker and Smirnoff. While alcohol sales surged during the lockdown of Covid-19, the subsequent decline of at-home consumption has weighed on the performance of Diageo. Concerns about the impact of tariffs were also a headwind during the quarter, as nearly half of the company’s US sales are imports from Mexico and Canada. We remain constructive on Diageo given its unique portfolio of brands, diverse geographic footprint and focus on premium spirits.

France’s Sodexo provides food services and catering to businesses, government agencies and schools in the US and Europe, with a smaller footprint in Asia. The company reported worse-than-expected results for its most recent quarter and lowered its forward guidance, citing slower growth in its US education segment and a modest decline in its retention rate. We believe Sodexo is a well-managed, highly cash flow generative business with defensive attributes.

German chemical company Henkel maintains leading global positions across both its industrial (adhesives) and consumer businesses (beauty care, laundry and home care). The company is experiencing weakness in both its industrial- and consumer-end markets and provided lower-than-expected sales guidance. During the quarter, the company announced the divestment of its North American private label business as it optimizes its portfolio to focus on businesses with better pricing power.

Bakkafrost is the third-largest fish-farming company in the world. The company operates vertically integrated salmon farms, primarily in the Faroe Islands, spanning from feed and smolt production—the stage in which salmon are ready to transition to the sea—to slaughter and processing. Higher harvest volumes across the industry and weakening consumer demand weighed on both salmon prices

and the shares of Bakkafrost during the quarter, as did concerns about potential tariffs on US imports. In our view, Bakkafrost is well positioned to benefit from longer-term growth in global seafood consumption and is insulated by the structural constraints on salmon farming.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support.

Sincerely,

First Eagle Investments

Definitions

Federal funds rate is the interest rate at which depository institutions (banks and credit unions) lend reserve balances to other depository institutions overnight on an uncollateralized basis. Gross domestic product (GDP) measures the value of total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

MSCI World Index (net) measures the performance of large and midcap equities across developed markets countries. A net-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income net of withholding taxes. MSCI EAFE Index (net) measures the performance of large and midcap equities across developed markets countries around the world excluding the US and Canada. A net-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income net of withholding taxes. NFIB Small Business Optimism Index is a widely recognized economic indicator measuring the sentiment and outlook of US small business owners across a variety of areas critical to their operations. A purchasing managers’ index (PMI) measures the growth or expansion of certain segments of the economy. S&P 500 Index (Gross/Total) measures the performance of 500 of the top companies in the leading industries of the US economy and is widely recognized as a proxy for the US market as a whole. A total-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income. Nikkei 225 is a price-weighted index composed of 225 stocks in the Prime Market of the Tokyo Stock Exchange. It is widely recognized as a proxy for the Japanese equity market as a whole. German DAX® Index measures the performance of the 40 largest companies listed on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange that fulfil certain minimum quality and profitability requirements. It is widely recognized as a proxy for the German equity market as a whole. CAC 40® Index is a free-float market capitalization-weighted index that measures the performance of the 40 largest and most actively traded shares listed on Euronext Paris.

Indices are unmanaged and do not incur management fees or other operating expenses. One cannot invest directly in an index.
